# How to Authenticate

Pass your API key correctly on every request.

# How to Authenticate

Every request to the Widgets API must include a bearer token in the
`Authorization` header. This page explains how to obtain a key and attach
it to your requests. For the full auth scheme spec (scopes, error codes,
token expiry), see the [API Reference](/api-reference).

## Get an API key

Sign in to your dashboard and navigate to **Settings → API Keys**. Click
**Create key**, give it a name, and copy the value — it won't be shown again.

## Attach the token

Pass your key as a bearer token on every request:

```bash
curl https://api.example.com/v1/widgets \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"
```

## Common errors

| Status | Code | Cause |
|--------|------|-------|
| 401 | `unauthorized` | Missing or malformed `Authorization` header |
| 401 | `unauthorized` | Key has been revoked |

If you receive a 401, double-check that the header value is exactly
`Bearer <key>` with no extra whitespace.

---

# Components and Symbols

How to use shared UI components, reusable snippets, and text variables in MDX.

# Components and Symbols

## UI components

Import from `docs-ui` in any MDX file:

```mdx
import { Callout, Tabs, Tab, CodeGroup, Kbd, Icon } from "docs-ui"
```

### Callout

```mdx
<Callout type="tip">Pro tip: do the thing.</Callout>
<Callout type="warning">This will overwrite existing data.</Callout>
```

Types: `tip`, `warning`, `note`, `danger`.

### Tabs and CodeGroup

```mdx
<Tabs>
  <Tab label="pnpm">```bash
pnpm install
```</Tab>
  <Tab label="npm">```bash
npm install
```</Tab>
</Tabs>
```

### Kbd

Inline keyboard shortcut display:

```mdx
Press <Kbd>⌘K</Kbd> to open search.
Press <Kbd>Ctrl</Kbd>+<Kbd>C</Kbd> to copy.
```

### Icon

Pass any [Lucide](https://lucide.dev/icons/) icon component:

```mdx
import { Icon } from "docs-ui"
import { ExternalLink, AlertTriangle, CheckCircle } from "lucide-react"

<Icon icon={ExternalLink} />
<Icon icon={AlertTriangle} size={20} />
```

Default size is 16px. Browse available icons at lucide.dev/icons.

---

## Text variables

Short strings that should be consistent across all docs — product name,
current version, URLs — live in `apps/docs/lib/vars.ts`:

```ts
export const vars = {
  productName: "Pepa",
  version: "1.0.0",
  repoUrl: "https://github.com/your-org/pepa",
}
```

Use them in MDX:

```mdx
import { vars } from "@/lib/vars"

**{vars.productName}** version {vars.version} introduced this feature.
```

To add a variable: add a key to `vars` in `lib/vars.ts`. It's immediately
available everywhere it's imported.

---

## Content snippets

Snippets are reusable content components — anything from a sentence to a full
code block — defined in `packages/ui/src/snippets/`. Edit the component once
and it updates in every doc that uses it.

```mdx
import { AuthNote, BearerExample } from "docs-ui/snippets"

<AuthNote />
<BearerExample />
```

### Adding a snippet

1. Create `packages/ui/src/snippets/MySnippet.tsx`:

```tsx
export function MySnippet() {
  return <p>Content that appears identically in every doc that uses it.</p>
}
```

2. Export it from `packages/ui/src/snippets/index.tsx`:

```ts
export { MySnippet } from "./MySnippet"
```

3. Import and use it in any MDX file:

```mdx
import { MySnippet } from "docs-ui/snippets"
<MySnippet />
```

Snippets can contain anything a React component can: prose, code blocks,
callouts, tables, other components. Keep them focused — if a snippet grows
into a full page's worth of content, it should become a real doc instead.

---

# Frontmatter Reference

Every frontmatter field, what it does, and when to use it.

Every `.mdx` file in `content/docs/` must have a frontmatter block. Fields
are validated at build time — an invalid or missing required field fails the
build with a clear error message pointing at the file.

## Required fields

### `title`

```yaml
title: How to Paginate Results
```

Shown as the page `<h1>`, in the browser tab, and in search results.
Do not repeat it as a heading inside the page body.

## Optional fields

### `description`

```yaml
description: Step-by-step guide to cursor-based pagination in the Widgets API.
```

One sentence. Shown in gray under the title and used as the meta description
for search engines.

### `type`

```yaml
type: how-to
```

The Diátaxis content type. Drives rendering hints and helps readers know what
to expect before they start reading.

| Value | When to use |
|-------|-------------|
| `tutorial` | Teaching something — reader is learning |
| `how-to` | Solving a specific problem — reader has a goal |
| `reference` | Factual lookup — reader knows what they want |
| `explanation` | Background understanding — reader wants context |
| `quickstart` | Fastest path to a working result |
| `changelog` | Release notes |
| `faq` | Question-and-answer format |
| `glossary` | Term definitions |
| `cookbook` | Copy-pasteable recipes, minimal prose |
| `architecture` | How the system itself is built (contributor-facing) |
| `migration` | Version upgrade instructions |

### `section`

```yaml
section: docs
```

Which top-level tab this page belongs to. Must match a section `id` in
`pepa.config.ts`.

| Value | Tab |
|-------|-----|
| `docs` | Main docs tab (default) |
| `api-reference` | API Reference tab |
| `cookbook` | Cookbook tab |
| `contributors` | Contributors tab |

### `nav`

```yaml
nav: How-to Guides
```

The sidebar group label within the section. Valid values:

`Tutorials` · `How-to Guides` · `Reference` · `Explanation` ·
`Quickstart` · `Changelog` · `FAQ` · `Glossary`

### `order`

```yaml
order: 3
```

Integer. Position within the sidebar group — lower numbers sort first.
Pages with the same `order` sort alphabetically by title.

### `status`

```yaml
status: beta
```

Adds a colored badge next to the page title. Omit this field for stable pages —
stable is the default and gets no badge.

| Value | Badge color | When to use |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| `beta` | Blue | Feature works but the API or content may change |
| `experimental` | Orange | May change significantly or be removed |
| `deprecated` | Red | Will be removed; link to the replacement |

### `hidden`

```yaml
hidden: true
```

Excludes the page from the sidebar and nav tree. The page still exists at its
URL and is included in the build — useful for landing pages or pages linked
from elsewhere but not meant to appear in navigation.

---

# Contributor Overview

How this documentation platform works and where everything lives.

This tab is for people maintaining the docs, not for end users of the product.
It covers the platform's own mechanics: how content is structured, what tools
are available, and how to use them.

## Repo layout

```
apps/docs/
  content/docs/      MDX source files — this is where you write
  components/        React components (layout, sidebar, TOC, MDX renderer)
  lib/               nav.ts, vars.ts — shared logic and text variables
  scripts/           CLI tools: find-replace, search index, llms.txt
  openapi/           OpenAPI spec source (copied to public/ at build time)
  pepa.config.ts     Enable/disable top-level nav tabs and features
  content-collections.ts  Frontmatter schema — build fails on invalid fields

packages/ui/src/
  Callout.tsx, Tabs.tsx, CodeGroup.tsx, Kbd.tsx, Icon.tsx
  snippets/          Reusable content blocks (edit once, updates everywhere)
```

## How the nav is built

There is no nav config file. The sidebar is derived entirely from frontmatter:

- `section` determines which top-level tab the page appears under
- `nav` determines which sidebar group it belongs to within that tab
- `order` sets the position within the group

Adding a page to the right place in the nav is just a matter of setting
the correct `section`, `nav`, and `order` in its frontmatter.

## How builds work

```bash
pnpm dev     # starts Next.js dev server at localhost:3000
pnpm build   # full static export into out/
```

The prebuild step runs automatically before `pnpm build`:

1. Copies `openapi/` → `public/openapi/` (so the spec is served statically)
2. Runs content-collections (validates frontmatter, compiles MDX)
3. Generates `public/search-index.json`
4. Generates `public/llms.txt` and `public/llms-full.txt` for AI agents

A missing required frontmatter field (like `title`) fails the build
immediately with a clear error — that's intentional.

---

# Style Linting

How to run the Google Developer Style Guide linter and interpret its output.

Pepa uses [Vale](https://vale.sh) with the
[Google Developer Style Guide](https://developers.google.com/style) ruleset
to catch common style violations before they reach production.

## First-time setup

```bash
brew install vale      # install the Vale binary (once per machine)
pnpm vale:sync         # download the Google style rules (once per machine)
```

`vale:sync` downloads rules into `.vale/styles/` at the repo root.
That directory is gitignored — run `vale:sync` once after cloning.

## Running the linter

From the repo root:

```bash
pnpm lint:style                                              # all docs
pnpm lint:style apps/docs/content/docs/getting-started.mdx  # single file
```

## Reading the output

```
apps/docs/content/docs/getting-started.mdx
 14:12  warning  Use 'select' instead of 'click on'.  Google.We
 27:4   warning  Avoid using 'simply'.                Google.Simplicity
```

Each line shows: `line:col  severity  message  rule-name`.

Severity levels (configured in `.vale.ini`):

| Level | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `suggestion` | Worth considering; low urgency |
| `warning` | Likely a style issue; fix before merging |
| `error` | Must fix |

The current config (`MinAlertLevel = suggestion`) shows all levels.

## Common violations

| Vale says | Fix |
|-----------|-----|
| "click on" | Use "click" |
| "simply", "easy", "just" | Remove — they're dismissive |
| Passive voice detected | Rewrite to active voice |
| Heading not in sentence case | Capitalize only the first word and proper nouns |
| Oxford comma missing | Add the comma before "and" in a list |
| Future tense ("will") | Use present tense where possible |

## Adjusting the rules

The config lives at `.vale.ini` in the repo root. To raise the minimum
severity to warnings only:

```ini
MinAlertLevel = warning
```

To disable a specific rule for a file, add a comment:

```mdx
<!-- vale Google.Simplicity = NO -->
This is simply unavoidable.
<!-- vale Google.Simplicity = YES -->
```

---

# Running a Term Sweep

Find and interactively replace a term across all docs in one pass.

Use the sweep tool when a term, product name, or phrase needs to be updated
across multiple docs at once. It shows every match in context and lets you
decide per-match whether to accept the change.

## Basic usage

From `apps/docs/`:

```bash
pnpm find-replace "old term" "new term"
```

This opens an interactive session. For each match you'll see:

```
content/docs/getting-started.mdx — match 1 of 4
   3 │
   4 │ This guide uses old term extensively.
   5 │

  - This guide uses old term extensively.
  + This guide uses new term extensively.

Accept? [y]es / [n]o / [a]ll / [q]uit:
```

## Keys

| Key | Action |
|-----|--------|
| `y` | Accept this match, move to the next |
| `n` | Skip this match, leave it unchanged |
| `a` | Accept this match and all remaining matches |
| `q` | Quit immediately, writing only the matches accepted so far |

## Preview without changing anything

```bash
pnpm find-replace "old term" "new term" --dry-run
```

Prints every match with context. Writes nothing. Useful for auditing scope
before committing to a sweep.

## Accept all without reviewing

```bash
pnpm find-replace "old term" "new term" --write
```

Accepts every match without prompting. Use this when you're confident the
substitution is always correct (e.g. a product rename with no ambiguous cases).

## Regex mode

```bash
pnpm find-replace "v(\d+\.\d+)" "version $1" --regex
```

Treats the first argument as a JavaScript regex. Capture groups are available
in the replacement as `$1`, `$2`, etc. Combine with `--write` or `--dry-run`
as needed.

## Scope

The sweep only touches `.mdx` files under `content/docs/`. It never modifies
app code, scripts, or configuration files.

---

# Authenticate a request

Add a bearer token to every API request.

**Goal:** Make an authenticated request to the Widgets API.

## The code

```bash
curl https://api.example.com/v1/widgets \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"
```

```js
const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/v1/widgets", {
  headers: {
    Authorization: `Bearer ${process.env.API_KEY}`,
  },
});
const { data } = await res.json();
```

```python
import os, httpx

resp = httpx.get(
    "https://api.example.com/v1/widgets",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['API_KEY']}"},
)
resp.raise_for_status()
data = resp.json()["data"]
```

## What to change

- Replace `YOUR_API_KEY` / `process.env.API_KEY` / `os.environ['API_KEY']`
  with however your app loads secrets.
- Replace the URL with your real API base URL.

---

# Cookbook

Copy-pasteable recipes for common tasks. Minimal explanation, maximum code.

import { Callout } from "docs-ui"

Recipes are short, complete, and runnable. Each one solves exactly one problem.
For background on *why* something works the way it does, see the main docs.

<Callout type="tip">
  Every recipe uses the Widgets API as the example. Swap in your own endpoints
  and the patterns transfer directly.
</Callout>

## What's here

| Recipe | What it covers |
|--------|---------------|
| [Authenticate a request](/cookbook/authenticate-a-request) | Add a bearer token to every request |
| [Paginate through results](/cookbook/paginate-results) | Walk a cursor-paginated list endpoint |
| [Handle errors](/cookbook/handle-errors) | Branch on status codes, surface error messages |

## How recipes are structured

Each recipe has:

1. **The goal** — one sentence
2. **The code** — complete, copy-pasteable
3. **What to change** — the two or three lines you'll need to adapt

Nothing else. If you want context, the main docs have it.

---

# Paginate through results

Walk a cursor-paginated list endpoint until all records are fetched.

**Goal:** Fetch every widget across multiple pages using cursor-based pagination.

## The code

```js
async function fetchAllWidgets(apiKey) {
  const widgets = [];
  let cursor = null;

  do {
    const url = new URL("https://api.example.com/v1/widgets");
    url.searchParams.set("limit", "100");
    if (cursor) url.searchParams.set("cursor", cursor);

    const res = await fetch(url, {
      headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${apiKey}` },
    });
    const body = await res.json();

    widgets.push(...body.data);
    cursor = body.next_cursor;
  } while (cursor);

  return widgets;
}
```

```python
import httpx

def fetch_all_widgets(api_key: str) -> list:
    widgets, cursor = [], None

    while True:
        params = {"limit": 100}
        if cursor:
            params["cursor"] = cursor

        resp = httpx.get(
            "https://api.example.com/v1/widgets",
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}"},
            params=params,
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        body = resp.json()

        widgets.extend(body["data"])
        cursor = body.get("next_cursor")
        if not cursor:
            break

    return widgets
```

## What to change

- `limit` — max is 100; lower it if responses are slow or payloads are large.
- The `next_cursor` field name — check your API's response schema if it differs.

---

# Feature Showcase

A living demo of every component and platform feature. Use this as a reference when authoring.

This page demonstrates every component available to doc authors.
The `status: beta` badge in the header above is showing off that feature.

No imports needed — all components are globally registered.

---

## Callouts

<Callout type="tip">
  Use `type="tip"` for helpful hints that aren't critical.
</Callout>

<Callout type="warning">
  Use `type="warning"` for things that can go wrong.
</Callout>

## Tabs

<Tabs>
  <Tab label="pnpm">
    ```bash
    pnpm install && pnpm dev
    ```
  </Tab>
  <Tab label="npm">
    ```bash
    npm install && npm run dev
    ```
  </Tab>
  <Tab label="yarn">
    ```bash
    yarn && yarn dev
    ```
  </Tab>
</Tabs>

## Syntax-highlighted code

```typescript
async function fetchWidgets(apiKey: string) {
  const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/v1/widgets", {
    headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${apiKey}` },
  });
  return res.json();
}
```

## Tables

| Component | MDX tag | What it does |
|-----------|---------|--------------|
| Callout | `<Callout type="tip">` | Tips, warnings, notes |
| Tabs / Tab | `<Tabs><Tab label="...">` | Multi-tab content |
| CodeGroup | `<CodeGroup>` | Grouped code blocks |
| Kbd | `<Kbd>⌘K</Kbd>` | Keyboard shortcut styling |
| Icon | `<Icon icon={...} />` | Inline Lucide icons |
| Var | `<Var name="productName" />` | Text variable |
| AuthNote | `<AuthNote />` | Reusable auth reminder snippet |

## Keyboard shortcuts

Press <Kbd>⌘K</Kbd> to open search.
Press <Kbd>⌘</Kbd>+<Kbd>Shift</Kbd>+<Kbd>P</Kbd> for the command palette.

## Icons

<LucideCircleCheck size={16} /> Success &nbsp;
<LucideTriangleAlert size={16} /> Warning &nbsp;
<LucideInfo size={16} /> Info &nbsp;
<LucideLink size={16} /> Link

Any icon from [lucide.dev/icons](https://lucide.dev/icons) is available as
`<Lucide{IconName} />` — no import required.

## Text variables

Product name: **<Var name="productName" />** · Version: **<Var name="version" />**

Defined in `lib/vars.ts` — change once, updates everywhere.

## Reusable snippet

`<AuthNote />` is defined once in `packages/ui/src/snippets/AuthNote.tsx`:

<AuthNote />

## Status badges

Add to any page's frontmatter — stable pages get no badge:

```yaml
status: beta          # blue
status: experimental  # orange
status: deprecated    # red
```

## Steps

Numbered steps with a connecting line. Code blocks, callouts, and any other
content can live inside a step.

<Steps>
  <Step title="Install dependencies">
    ```bash
    pnpm install
    ```
  </Step>
  <Step title="Configure your project">
    Edit `apps/docs/pepa.config.ts` to set your project name and enable the
    sections you need.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Add your first doc">
    Create an `.mdx` file under `content/docs/` with the required frontmatter,
    then run `pnpm dev` to see it appear in the sidebar.

    ```yaml
    ---
    title: My First Page
    nav: Tutorials
    order: 1
    ---
    ```
  </Step>
  <Step title="Deploy">
    Push to `main`. GitHub Actions builds the static export and publishes it
    to GitHub Pages automatically.
  </Step>
</Steps>

## Tiles

Cards arranged in a responsive grid. A single tile expands to fill the full
width automatically.

<Tiles>
  <Tile title="Tutorials" iconName="BookOpen" href="/getting-started">
    Learning-oriented guides that walk you through a first success.
  </Tile>
  <Tile title="API Reference" iconName="Code" href="/api-reference">
    Auto-generated from your OpenAPI spec via Scalar.
  </Tile>
  <Tile title="Cookbook" iconName="ChefHat" href="/cookbook/overview">
    Copy-pasteable recipes — minimal explanation, maximum code.
  </Tile>
  <Tile title="Contributors" iconName="Users" href="/contributors/overview">
    Platform docs for maintainers: sweeps, style linting, and components.
  </Tile>
</Tiles>

A single tile fills the full row:

<Tiles>
  <Tile title="Single tile" iconName="Zap" href="/getting-started">
    One tile always expands to the full content width.
  </Tile>
</Tiles>

## Heading anchor links

Hover any heading — a chain-link icon appears. Click to copy the direct URL.

---

# Getting Started

Install the platform and write your first doc.

import { Callout } from "docs-ui";

This is a sample page to prove the content pipeline works end to end:
frontmatter validation, MDX compilation, syntax highlighting, and a custom
component imported from the shared `docs-ui` package.

<Callout type="tip">
  Edit this file at `content/docs/getting-started.mdx` — saving it hot-reloads
  in dev and re-validates against the schema in `content-collections.ts`.
</Callout>

## Install

```bash
pnpm install
pnpm dev
```

## Why the schema matters

If you delete the `title` field above and save, the build fails immediately
with a clear error pointing at this file — instead of shipping a docs page
with a blank `<h1>` to production. That's the whole value of a typed content
layer over parsing MDX by hand.
